title: SARS-CoV-2: Disease Prevalence, Severity, Associated Risk Factors and Predictors in the Accra Metropolis
author: Aboagye, Frank Twum
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Post-lockdown studies on the epidemiological profile of SARS-CoV-2 in Ghana have been limited. More so, adherence to preventive measures has significantly reduced and this presents for the resurgence of the disease as has been observed in several sub-Sahara African countries. This study determined the prevalence and severity of SARS-CoV-2 infection and its associated risk factors and predictors within the Accra Metropolis. This was a prospective cross-sectional study conducted between June and August 2022. Nasopharyngeal samples were collected from 268 consenting participants and were subjected to nucleic acid extraction and followed by real-time polymerase chain reaction for the detection and quantification of SARS-CoV-2 RNA. The prevalence of SARS-CoV-2 detected within the Accra Metropolis was 30.2% (CI 95 : 24.7 – 35.8). Majority of the SARS-CoV-2 infection was diagnosed in females, participants aged 41 – 50 years, and symptomatic participants. Participants aged 10 years and below and females recorded the highest viral load with the highest number of infections recorded in participants aged 41-50 years. Furthermore, there was a significant inverse relationship between the Ct-values of the E-gene, N-gene, and RdRP gene and their corresponding viral load. Predictors of SARS-CoV-2 infection identified were chills, cough, headache, body weakness, sore throat, and dyspnoea in order of decreasing association with SARS-CoV-2 infection. There was a strong association between symptom status, gender, and age and SARS-CoV-2 infection. The findings of the study denote a high prevalence of SARS-CoV-2 within the Accra Metropolis Persons presenting with symptoms related to SARS-CoV-2 infection are most likely to be diagnosed with COVID-19. Attention should be paid to persons presenting with symptoms to help contain the virus as most preventive measures have currently been relaxed. Education on preventive measures and vaccination should thus be intensified.
